take_off: The Airbus A320 begins its takeoff roll at Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, positioned at latitude 49.00551°N and longitude 2.55497°E. At precisely the moment of departure, 0 minutes since start, the aircraft is stationary on the runway, aligned on a heading of 264°, with a ground speed of just 12 knots and no vertical speed. The environmental conditions at this early hour are stable: turbulence indices at 300 hPa show low values (EDR 0.022 m²/³s⁻¹, Ellrod 1.6 s⁻²), and the Richardson number is a high 10.0, indicating a stable atmosphere. Convective Available Potential Energy (CAPE) is zero, suggesting no convective activity. Winds at flight level are brisk from the north-northeast (31.1 m/s at 13.9°), while surface winds are light and from the east-northeast (1.8 m/s at 63.6°). The aircraft sits poised on the tarmac, just northeast of Paris, with the city’s lights stretching to the west.  Nearly four minutes later, at 3.9 minutes since departure, the A320 has taxied northward to 49.01826°N, 2.55130°E, still at ground level and maintaining a ground speed of 11 knots. The heading has shifted to 351°, suggesting a turn as the aircraft lines up for takeoff, perhaps holding short or awaiting clearance. Meteorological conditions remain unchanged, with turbulence and wind parameters steady, and the air over the Île-de-France region remains calm and stable.  A significant change occurs as the aircraft accelerates and lifts off. At 12.1 minutes after departure, the A320 is airborne, climbing rapidly at 2,176 ft/min. Now at 4,675 ft above sea level, the aircraft is positioned at 49.03119°N, 2.71370°E, tracking 85°—almost due east—at a ground speed of 233 knots. The squawk code has changed to 1000, indicating entry into controlled airspace. Turbulence remains minimal (EDR 0.021, Ellrod 0.9), and atmospheric stability is still high (Richardson number 9.1). CAPE remains zero, and winds aloft are steady at 31.5 m/s from 13.4°. Surface winds have picked up slightly to 2.3 m/s, veering to 66.3°. The aircraft climbs out over the eastern suburbs of Paris, crossing the Marne River valley as it leaves the urban sprawl behind.  Four minutes later, at 16.1 minutes since departure, the A320 has climbed to 15,025 ft, now well above the Paris Basin. Its position is 48.70267°N, 2.70488°E, tracking 196°—a sharp turn to the south-southwest—at a ground speed of 390 knots and a vertical speed of 2,752 ft/min, indicating a strong climb. The turbulence indices remain low (EDR 0.021, Ellrod 1.3), and the Richardson number is 9.3, still indicative of a stable atmosphere. Winds at 10,000 meters remain steady at 31.5 m/s, now from 13.6°, while surface winds are 2.5 m/s from 69.3°. The aircraft is now passing over the rural landscapes of northern France, with the Seine River meandering to the west.  By 20.1 minutes into the flight, the A320 has reached 23,550 ft at 48.24669°N, 2.73574°E, heading almost due south (track 170°) at 442 knots. The rate of climb has eased to 1,344 ft/min as the aircraft approaches its cruise altitude. Turbulence remains benign (EDR 0.022, Ellrod 0.9), and atmospheric stability is still high (Richardson number 9.0). Winds aloft have increased slightly to 31.9 m/s from 12.5°, and surface winds are 2.7 m/s from 63.7°. The flight now traverses the rolling fields and forests of the Centre-Val de Loire region, with the Loire River to the west.  As the climb continues, at 24.1 minutes after takeoff, the aircraft is at 29,500 ft, located at 47.76069°N, 2.98201°E, tracking 157° (south-southeast) at 484 knots, with a vertical speed of 960 ft/min. The turbulence indices have dropped further (EDR 0.017, Ellrod 0.4), and the Richardson number is 8.8, still reflecting stable conditions. Winds at altitude are steady at 31.7 m/s from 11.6°, and surface winds are 2.6 m/s from 71.0°. The A320 is now above the northern edge of the Massif Central, a region of ancient volcanic hills and forests.  At 28.1 minutes since departure, the aircraft has climbed to 34,825 ft at 47.25343°N, 3.29803°E, maintaining a track of 157° and a ground speed of 492 knots. The vertical speed is now 896 ft/min, indicating the final stages of climb. Turbulence remains low (EDR 0.017, Ellrod 1.1), though the Richardson number has decreased to 7.0, suggesting a slight reduction in atmospheric stability but still well within safe margins. Winds at 10,000 meters have increased to 32.7 m/s from 10.6°, and surface winds are 3.5 m/s from 63.2°. The aircraft is now over the eastern reaches of the Massif Central, with the Loire River valley stretching westward.  Finally, at 32.1 minutes into the flight, the A320 levels off at its initial cruise altitude of 37,000 ft, positioned at 46.93569°N, 3.86895°E, tracking 121°—a turn to the southeast—at a ground speed of 458 knots. The vertical speed has dropped to zero as the aircraft transitions to cruise. Turbulence indices have increased slightly (EDR 0.023, Ellrod 1.8), but remain within comfortable limits, and the Richardson number has rebounded to 9.4, indicating a return to greater atmospheric stability. Winds aloft are now 34.6 m/s from 9.2°, and surface winds remain steady at 3.5 m/s from 60.5°. The aircraft is now high above the rolling hills and forests of Burgundy, with the Saône River to the east and the distant Alps just beginning to appear on the horizon. The A320 has completed its climb, and the flight transitions smoothly into the cruise phase, having left the Paris region far behind and now soaring above the heart of France.
cruising: The Airbus A320 commences its cruise phase high above central France, with the first datapoint marking the aircraft precisely at 0 minutes into cruise. At this moment, the jet is maintaining a steady altitude of 37,000 feet, its position fixed at latitude 46.93569 and longitude 3.86895—just east of Nevers, in the heart of the Burgundy region. The aircraft tracks southeast on a heading of 121°, slicing through the upper troposphere at a brisk ground speed of 458 knots. The vertical speed is zero, indicating level flight, while the squawk code remains set to 1000. Meteorological conditions are favorable: the EDR at 300 hPa is a low 0.023, and the Ellrod index is 1.8, both suggesting only light turbulence. The Richardson number, at 9.4, points to a stable atmosphere, and CAPE is negligible, indicating little convective activity. Winds at 10,000 meters are from the north (9.2°) at 34.6 m/s, while surface winds are gentle from the northeast.  Four minutes into the cruise, the A320 has progressed southeast to latitude 46.66562, longitude 4.49401, now east of Chalon-sur-Saône and nearing the Saône River valley. The aircraft maintains its altitude—just 25 feet below the initial level—and its heading shifts slightly to 122°, with ground speed unchanged. The vertical speed remains zero, confirming continued level flight. Turbulence remains light, with EDR at 0.025 and Ellrod index dropping to 0.5. Atmospheric stability improves, as the Richardson number rises to 10.0. Winds aloft have picked up slightly to 35.1 m/s, still from the north, while surface winds remain light.  By 8 minutes into the cruise, the aircraft is approaching the foothills of the Jura Mountains, near latitude 46.39151 and longitude 5.11474, east of Bourg-en-Bresse. The heading holds steady at 122°, and the altitude returns to 37,000 feet, with a slight positive vertical speed of 64 ft/min—barely perceptible to those onboard. The ground speed remains at 458 knots. Turbulence indicators remain benign: EDR drops to 0.018, though the Ellrod index briefly rises to 2.0, still within light turbulence thresholds. The Richardson number remains high at 10.0, and CAPE is still absent. Winds at altitude have increased to 37.0 m/s, now from 10°, while surface winds are slightly stronger at 4.0 m/s from the north-northeast.  As the A320 continues its southeastward journey, 12 minutes into the flight, it passes near Oyonnax and the edge of the French Alps at latitude 46.11353, longitude 5.72752. The heading shifts subtly to 127°, and the aircraft climbs marginally to 37,025 feet before descending again, with a gentle vertical speed of -64 ft/min. Ground speed edges up to 461 knots. Turbulence remains minimal, with EDR at 0.016 and Ellrod index at 1.5. The Richardson number holds at 10.0, indicating persistent atmospheric stability. Winds aloft are now 37.8 m/s from 7.5°, while surface winds drop to 1.3 m/s from the southwest.  At 16 minutes, the A320 is deep within the Alpine region, near latitude 45.75131 and longitude 6.25568, just west of Albertville and the iconic Mont Blanc massif. The aircraft now tracks 135°, maintaining 37,000 feet and a ground speed of 476 knots. The vertical speed is zero, and the squawk code remains unchanged. Turbulence is still light, though the Ellrod index briefly peaks at 3.2, but the EDR is a low 0.017. The Richardson number is steady at 10.0, and CAPE remains negligible. Winds at cruising altitude strengthen to 39.3 m/s from 6.6°, while surface winds are nearly calm.  Four minutes later, at 20 minutes into cruise, the A320 crosses into Italy, flying southeast over the Susa Valley near latitude 45.37987 and longitude 6.79337, with the snow-capped peaks of the Western Alps below. The heading is 134°, altitude climbs slightly to 37,025 feet, and ground speed increases to 484 knots. A gentle climb is indicated by a vertical speed of 64 ft/min. Turbulence remains light (EDR 0.019, Ellrod 1.5), and the Richardson number is still a robust 10.0. Winds at 10,000 meters decrease to 28.1 m/s from 5.7°, while surface winds are light from the southwest.  By 24 minutes, the aircraft is southeast of Turin, near latitude 45.00177 and longitude 7.33746, maintaining a heading of 134°. The altitude holds at 37,025 feet, but a slight descent begins with a vertical speed of -64 ft/min, and ground speed is 471 knots. Here, the EDR spikes to 0.083, indicating a brief patch of moderate turbulence, while the Ellrod index is 1.6. The Richardson number dips to 6.6, suggesting a localized reduction in atmospheric stability. Winds at altitude increase sharply to 45.2 m/s from 5.4°, while surface winds remain light.  Approaching the Ligurian Alps at 28 minutes, the A320 is at latitude 44.63708, longitude 7.85221, tracking 135°. The aircraft returns to 37,000 feet, with ground speed at 478 knots and level flight. Turbulence subsides (EDR 0.031, Ellrod 0.2), and the Richardson number remains at 6.6. Winds aloft are strong at 46.1 m/s from 7.3°, with surface winds still calm.  At 32 minutes, the aircraft is over the rolling hills of southern Piedmont, near latitude 44.25696 and longitude 8.37832, maintaining heading and altitude. Ground speed is 482 knots. A notable increase in EDR to 0.137 suggests a short-lived encounter with moderate turbulence, while the Ellrod index is 0.5. The Richardson number rises to 7.9, and CAPE appears for the first time at 0.039, hinting at some convective potential. Winds at 10,000 meters are 41.4 m/s from 7.5°, and surface winds pick up to 1.9 m/s from the northwest.  Continuing southeast, 36 minutes into cruise, the A320 passes over the Ligurian coast near Savona, at latitude 43.87381 and longitude 8.89893. The heading is unchanged, altitude holds at 37,025 feet, and ground speed is 480 knots. Turbulence is light (EDR 0.042, Ellrod 1.3), but the Richardson number drops to 6.2, and CAPE increases to 0.223, suggesting a slightly more unstable atmosphere. Winds at altitude are 42.8 m/s from 3.1°, while surface winds are stronger at 4.0 m/s from the east.  Finally, at 40 minutes into the cruise, the aircraft begins its descent over the Ligurian Sea, just west of Genoa, at latitude 43.4939 and longitude 9.40554. The heading is 136°, and altitude decreases to 35,700 feet, with a significant vertical speed of -960 ft/min marking the transition toward the descent phase. Ground speed remains high at 478 knots. Turbulence is light (EDR 0.038), though the Ellrod index rises to 2.2. The Richardson number returns to 10.0, indicating restored stability, and CAPE is elevated at 0.233. Winds at 10,000 meters are 37.3 m/s from 2.4°, while surface winds are 4.3 m/s from the north-northeast. The A320 now leaves the Italian coast behind, preparing for its approach over the blue expanse of the Mediterranean.
landing: The aircraft for this approach is an Airbus A320, renowned for its reliability and efficiency on short- to medium-haul routes. At the outset of this landing phase, precisely at the moment marking 0 minutes since the start of descent, the A320 is positioned at 43.4939°N, 9.40554°E, high above the Tyrrhenian Sea, just off the coast of Tuscany. The aircraft is tracking 136°, descending from a cruising altitude of 35,700 feet at a significant vertical speed of -960 feet per minute, and maintaining a robust ground speed of 478 knots. Meteorological conditions at this altitude are relatively benign, with a low EDR (0.038) and moderate Ellrod index (2.2), indicating only light turbulence. The Richardson number is a stable 10.0, and CAPE is modest at 0.233, suggesting little convective activity. Winds at 10,000 meters are brisk, blowing from the north at 37.3 m/s, while surface winds are light from the northeast.  Four minutes into the descent, the A320 has covered ground to 43.12138°N, 9.89338°E, still over the Tyrrhenian waters but drawing nearer to the Tuscan Archipelago. The aircraft maintains its southeast heading of 136°, now at 32,675 feet, with the descent rate increasing to -1,472 feet per minute and ground speed reducing to 458 knots. Turbulence remains light, with a slight uptick in EDR to 0.073, but the Ellrod index dips to 1.9. Atmospheric stability is unchanged, and the wind aloft, though still strong at 33.8 m/s from nearly due north, poses no significant challenge.  By eight minutes after the start of descent, the A320 has shifted course slightly to 108°, passing near the island of Elba at 42.78351°N, 10.38926°E. The descent continues, now at 26,525 feet and -1,408 feet per minute, with ground speed dropping to 404 knots. Turbulence indicators show a moderate increase in the Ellrod index to 3.9, though EDR remains low at 0.054. The atmosphere remains stable, with the Richardson number holding at 10.0. Winds at altitude ease to 28.4 m/s, still from the north, while CAPE decreases slightly, indicating continued lack of convective instability.  As the aircraft approaches the Tuscan coast twelve minutes into the descent, it adjusts heading to 119°, descending through 21,150 feet at a gentler -896 feet per minute. The position is now 42.59583°N, 10.93502°E, just east of the island of Giglio and approaching the Maremma region of mainland Italy. Ground speed stabilizes at 405 knots. Turbulence is minimal, with EDR at 0.042 and Ellrod index at 1.0, and the atmosphere remains stable and calm, with negligible CAPE. Winds at 10,000 meters continue to subside, now at 23.0 m/s.  By sixteen minutes after descent initiation, the A320 is at 42.39009°N, 11.41697°E, tracking 120°, and passing over the rolling hills of southern Tuscany. The aircraft is now at 17,325 feet, still descending at -896 feet per minute, and ground speed has decreased to 328 knots. Turbulence remains light (EDR 0.023, Ellrod 0.7), and the Richardson number is steady. Winds aloft have diminished further to 18.3 m/s, and surface winds remain light.  At the twenty-minute mark, the aircraft is at 42.20197°N, 11.85106°E, continuing on a 120° track, now descending through 14,125 feet at -1,024 feet per minute. The A320 is approaching the Lazio region, with the Tyrrhenian coastline and the city of Civitavecchia to the southeast. Ground speed is steady at 331 knots. Turbulence remains low, though the Ellrod index briefly spikes to 4.5, possibly reflecting some localized wind shear. Atmospheric stability is unchanged, and CAPE is negligible.  Four minutes later, at 24 minutes since descent began, the aircraft is at 42.01488°N, 12.22385°E, altering course to 141°, and descending through 9,675 feet at -1,024 feet per minute. The A320 is now inland, approaching the outskirts of Rome, with the Apennine foothills visible to the east. Ground speed drops to 280 knots. Turbulence remains light (EDR 0.019), though the Ellrod index rises to 9.2, indicating some increased shear, but the Richardson number remains stable, suggesting no significant threat to flight stability. Winds at altitude are now 16.1 m/s, and surface winds are gentle.  At 28 minutes, the aircraft is at 41.77721°N, 12.42984°E, tracking 162°, and descending through 6,200 feet. The descent rate briefly reverses to a slight climb (+128 feet per minute), possibly as the aircraft intercepts the glide slope for final approach into Rome Fiumicino Airport. Ground speed reduces to 220 knots. Turbulence indicators show a marked increase, with the Ellrod index at 18.0 and a slight drop in the Richardson number to 7.4, suggesting the presence of moderate wind shear in the lower atmosphere. Winds at altitude remain steady, and surface winds pick up to 8.2 m/s from the northeast.  As the aircraft continues its approach, at 32 minutes it is at 41.55862°N, 12.4849°E, now tracking 234°, descending through 2,850 feet at -896 feet per minute, and maintaining a ground speed of 224 knots. The A320 is now on the final approach path, just southwest of central Rome, with the Tiber River and the city’s historic landmarks visible in the distance. Turbulence remains moderate, with the Ellrod index steady at 18.0 and the Richardson number at 7.4.  At 36 minutes, the aircraft is at 41.70378°N, 12.32092°E, tracking 342°, and descending through 2,475 feet at -832 feet per minute, slowing to 170 knots. The approach path now curves northward, perhaps as the aircraft aligns with the runway for a final approach. Turbulence and wind conditions remain unchanged.  By 40 minutes since the start of descent, the A320 has touched down at 41.83067°N, 12.2674°E, on the runway at Rome Fiumicino Airport. The aircraft is now at ground level, with a ground speed of 38 knots as it decelerates along the runway. The Ellrod index remains high at 18.0, reflecting persistent wind shear near the surface, and the Richardson number is 7.4. Surface winds are moderate at 8.2 m/s from the northeast.  In the minutes following touchdown, the aircraft taxis to its assigned gate. At 44 minutes, it is at 41.80767°N, 12.2732°E, tracking 188°, moving at 26 knots. At 48 minutes, it is nearly stationary at 41.79806°N, 12.25948°E, and by 52 minutes, the A320 comes to a complete stop at 41.7981°N, 12.25935°E, with a ground speed of just 3 knots. Throughout the taxi phase, surface winds remain steady, and turbulence indicators are unchanged, suggesting a smooth and uneventful arrival at the gate.  In summary, this landing phase for the A320 is characterized by a steady, controlled descent from cruise altitude over the Tyrrhenian Sea, a smooth approach over the scenic landscapes of Tuscany and Lazio, and a final approach and touchdown at Rome Fiumicino Airport. Meteorological conditions throughout the descent are generally stable, with only moderate turbulence and wind shear encountered in the lower atmosphere, ensuring a safe and comfortable arrival for all on board.
